ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for water chestnut sugarcane blend; closest match is sugarcane juice food product. Water chestnut component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (sugar cane juice) used as closest defensible anchor; no specific code exists for water chestnut and sugar cane blended packaged beverage.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT code identified for this packaged herbal beverage blend.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one 250 ml can or bottle, equivalent to approximately 250 g, commonly consumed as a refreshing drink at hawker centres or purchased from convenience stores in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Water Chestnut Sugar Cane Drink — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged water chestnut sugar cane drink (cr_id D05010041). All core macros and secondary nutrients reflect the HPB-reported values at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged water chestnut sugar cane drink. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sugar cane juice (GI ~65–70, glucose reference). The water chestnut component contributes negligible starch in this diluted packaged form; the dominant glycaemic driver is the dissolved sucrose/glucose from sugar cane juice. A conservative midpoint of 65 is applied. GL per serving = (65 × 6.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 9.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6 g sugar per 100 g; insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain independently elevate blood pressure risk despite low sodium content of 15 mg per 100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage, as regular consumption of added sugar in liquid form promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, independently raising blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Liquid sugar (6 g per 100 g, all as sugar, zero dietary fibre) with estimated GI of 65; rapid postprandial glucose spike from sugar cane-derived sucrose and glucose in solution.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this beverage, as the 6 g of sugar per 100 g in liquid form — with zero dietary fibre to slow absorption — causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 15 g of rapidly absorbed sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation, weight gain, and systemic inflammation; this product contains 6 g sugar per 100 g with no protective nutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this sugar-sweetened packaged beverage, as habitual intake of liquid sugars is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and systemic inflammation, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, fibre, antioxidant vitamins) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage, as the added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation through pro-inflammatory mediators, and the drink provides no compensating anti-inflammatory nutrients such as omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or dietary fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Water Chestnut Sugar Cane Drink (Packaged) is a ready-to-drink sweetened beverage widely sold in Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically available in cans or bottles at hawker centres, convenience stores, and supermarkets. It is produced by blending water chestnut extract (from Eleocharis dulcis) with sugar cane juice (from Saccharum officinarum), resulting in a lightly sweet, mildly earthy, and refreshing cold drink. The packaged version is commercially processed and standardised, distinguishing it from freshly pressed hawker-stall variants. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie beverage at 24 kcal per 100 g, with all caloric content derived from 6 g of carbohydrate per 100 g — entirely as sugar — and negligible protein and fat. Dietary fibre is absent, and sodium is low at 15 mg per 100 g. Despite its natural ingredient origins, the sugar content in liquid form raises the glycaemic index to an estimated 65 (medium GI), and a standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 15 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with a glycaemic load of approximately 9.75. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid postprandial glucose elevation, and it is also a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management due to the independent cardiovascular risk associated with regular sugar-sweetened beverage consumption. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. The drink is culturally associated with traditional Chinese herbal cooling beverages and is commonly consumed as a thirst-quenching snack drink in tropical Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is water chestnut sugar cane drink healthy?
Can diabetics drink water chestnut sugarcane juice?
How many calories in a can of water chestnut sugar cane drink?
Is packaged sugarcane drink high in sugar?
What is the GI of sugar cane juice?
Is water chestnut drink good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010041) at confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sugar cane juice, which is the dominant glycaemic ingredient; this reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong nutrient provenance.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — this packaged beverage is formulated from water chestnut extract and sugar cane juice with no protein-contributing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — the product contains no fat-contributing ingredients by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — the filtration and processing of packaged cane juice removes fibre. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. calcium_mg and iron_mg are set to 0.0 as explicitly reported as zero by HPB. GI note: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sugar cane juice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific packaged product was found in approved sources. GL per serving calculated as (65 × 6.0 × 250) / 10000 = 9.75. Malay local name uses 'sengkuang' which in standard Malay refers to jicama; the more precise Malay term for water chestnut is 'keladi tikus' or 'sengkuang air' — the name used here reflects common Singaporean Malay commercial labelling convention.
